artifacts.content:
Hi Laura,

I just wanted to get this all down while it’s fresh and before you get home from the clinic.

Dr. Patel was pretty blunt today. He said the “primary issue is still the weak heart muscle from the March 18, 2024 heart attack at St. Mary’s” and reminded me my LVEF is sitting around 35–40%. He actually mentioned again how I blew off that October 2023 cardiology follow‑up because I was stuck doing those back‑to‑back overtime shifts at TriCity. Hearing him say that out loud, with you not there to translate the medical stuff, made me feel pretty stupid.

He went over the whole history: years of high blood pressure that he’d been warning me about since 2016, the diabetes that Dr. Choudhury has been chasing since that A1C of 9.4% back on March 12, 2023, and me going off the atorvastatin for most of 2022 after Mom’s muscle pain scared me even though my LDL was 4.8 on that March 3, 2022 call. He said none of it “caused” yesterday’s chest twinges on its own, but together they set the stage.

He also asked about how rehab at Grand River is going since December and whether I’m still getting winded walking to the mailbox and taking Mom in for dialysis. I told him even that 5–10 minute walk into Grand River twice a week can leave me at 6/10 chest pressure and gasping.

Anyway, nothing new medication‑wise, but he really wants me to stick with the CPAP and the sertraline and keep the sodium down. Can we go over the pill organizer again tonight? I keep second‑guessing whether I actually took the morning ones.

Love,
Michael

Hey Dave,

Figured I’d give you the longer version of what’s been going on, since we only ever seem to talk in five‑minute bursts between your shifts.

I keep thinking back to that “light duty” day at TriCity on September 9, 2024. Remember I told you they brought me back just to “walk the floor and observe”? I barely lasted two hours of slowly walking the aisles and climbing the mezzanine stairs before I nearly blacked out near loading dock 3. Mark Jenkins wrote it up in some internal note, which at the time felt embarrassing, but now I see it as the moment it was obvious I couldn’t do the physical side anymore.

The thing is, the warning signs were there long before. Thanksgiving 2023 at Sandra’s place in Waterloo, I had to stop after one trip carrying groceries up her front steps and lie down in her spare room for about 45 minutes while everyone else finished dinner. I shrugged it off as being “out of shape.”

Then came that panic‑like episode at the Zehrs on Ottawa Street on May 2, 2024. I was sure it was another heart attack in the freezer aisle. Paramedics from Waterloo Region EMS came, did the whole workup right there. Dr. Nguyen later called it a panic attack triggered by the angina and started me on sertraline 50 mg a day and sent me to the Grand River cardiac rehab program.

Now, since the big one on November 26, 2025, even walking Mom from the parking lot into Grand River for her dialysis twice a week leaves me with 6/10 chest pressure and short of breath for 10–15 minutes. That’s my “workday” now.

Sometimes I wish I’d listened in 2016 when Dr. Patel at Forest Hill was nagging me about the blood pressure instead of letting Mark Reynolds scare me out of those follow‑ups with the “better not miss another Saturday” speech.

Anyway, didn’t mean to dump all this on you. Just tired of pretending it’s all fine.

Mike

Personal health + work timeline (for my own head)

– June 2016: Dr. Patel at Forest Hill Family Practice starts warning me seriously about uncontrolled hypertension. I skip two follow‑up visits that summer because TriCity has me on mandatory Saturday overtime and Mark Reynolds basically says I “better not miss another Saturday” after I left early once for a BP check.

– Oct–Dec 2021: Peak season at TriCity, 120 Manitou Dr. Working 60–65 hrs/week, living on pizza and wings in the office, sneaking 3–4 cigarettes a shift with Jason Miller behind the loading dock even though I’d quit in 2018.

– Nov 2021: CPAP from the Sleep Disorders Clinic at St. Mary’s dies. Replacement quote is $780 out of pocket. I keep putting it off because of the line of credit and the arguments with Laura. End up about 18 months basically untreated for sleep apnea.

– Feb–Nov 2022: Stop taking atorvastatin after Mom (Margaret) has bad muscle pains on her statin. Ignore Dr. Patel’s March 3, 2022 phone warning about my LDL of 4.8 and “high risk for heart attack.”

– March 12, 2023: Endo visit with Dr. Anita Choudhury at Grand River – A1C 9.4% even after bumping metformin to 2000 mg/day and adding gliclazide MR 60 mg in January.

– Oct 9, 2023: Thanksgiving at Sandra’s in Waterloo – first obvious chest tightness and breathlessness carrying groceries; 45 minutes lying down in her spare room.

– March 18, 2024: LAD STEMI at St. Mary’s, PCI with drug‑eluting stent.

– Sept 9, 2024: “Light duty” near‑faint at loading dock 3.

– May 2, 2024: Panic episode at Zehrs Ottawa St; EMS called; Dr. Nguyen later starts sertraline 50 mg and refers to Grand River cardiac rehab.

– Nov 26, 2025: Big MI, ambulance to Grand River.

Remember: can’t change past, but this is why I can’t go back to 60‑hr warehouse weeks.

Event: Drive Mom to dialysis – Grand River Hospital
Date: Every Tuesday and Friday
Time: 8:00–10:15 a.m.
Location: Grand River Hospital – Dialysis Unit, Kitchener

Details / reminders for myself:

– Leave house on Maple Ridge Crescent by 8:00 a.m. to get Mom (Margaret) to her 8:30 a.m. slot.
– Park as close as possible; that 5–10 minute walk from the parking lot to the dialysis unit has been leaving me with about 6/10 chest pressure and pretty bad shortness of breath since early December 2025.
– Plan to sit for 10–15 minutes in the waiting area after getting her settled before driving home so I’m not pushing it.
– Bring water, nitroglycerin, and list of my own meds in case anything happens.

Notes on symptoms:

Lately I notice I’m already tired from poor sleep before we even leave. Since mid‑December I’ve been waking 2–3 times a night with my heart pounding and that dream about collapsing on the TriCity warehouse floor while arguing with Mark about the November 2025 overtime shifts I missed because of chest pain. Chest heaviness in those wake‑ups is about 5/10 but settles if I sit up and breathe.

If rehab at St. Mary’s the day before was hard, the Tuesday dialysis run feels worse. On those weeks, plan an afternoon nap (45–60 minutes) when I get back, similar to how wiped I feel after taking Ryan to his probation check‑ins at the Ontario Court of Justice on Frederick St.

Goal: By May 2026, be able to do this walk with less than 3/10 chest tightness.

Hi Emily,

Just finished my video session with Dr. Karen Li from Grand River Community Mental Health and thought I’d write while it’s all swirling around in my head.

We ended up talking a lot about you – in a good way, mostly. I told her how proud I am that you’re grinding through the McMaster nursing program in Waterloo, and also how guilty I’ve felt about not being able to help when you were short on rent back in November and juggling those student loan payments. Right after we finished, my heart did that fluttering thing again for about 45 seconds, same as it’s been doing most weekdays since early January. I had to sit quietly on the couch for 10 minutes until it settled.

She also asked about that Zehrs episode on Ottawa Street last May 2, when I was sure I was having another heart attack and the paramedics showed up in the frozen foods. I told her that’s when Dr. Nguyen started the sertraline 50 mg and sent me to the Grand River cardiac rehab program.

We went over some of the older stuff too – how I kept missing appointments when TriCity piled on the overtime, and how that October 2023 cardiology follow‑up with Dr. Patel got bumped for back‑to‑back shifts before Black Friday. It’s weird hearing it all laid out; makes it obvious how long I put work ahead of my health and, by extension, ahead of you and Ryan.

Anyway, I don’t want you worrying that you’re “stressing me out.” The truth is, talking about this helps, even if my heart flips around for a bit afterward. If you’re free Sunday, maybe you could come by, bring your laptop, and we’ll just sit at the kitchen table like we did last week.

Love you,
Dad

[Thread with Laura]

Michael: Just got back from Sandra’s. I need to tell you about New Year’s Eve before it gets fuzzy.

Laura: Okay… what happened? You sounded off when you called.

Michael: I was carrying two cases of beer and a 24‑pack of soda up the stairs to her kitchen. Halfway up I got this 6–7/10 chest tightness and shortness of breath. Had to duck into her spare bedroom and sit down. It lasted about 12–15 minutes until I took one nitro.

Laura: Why were you carrying that much? You’re not supposed to be hauling stuff like that.

Michael: I know. I was just trying to be “normal Uncle Mike” again. Everyone freaked because they remember how I used to drink way too much at those gatherings before the March 18, 2024 heart attack at St. Mary’s and now the big one in November.

Laura: Did anyone call 911?

Michael: No, I talked them out of it. It felt like the exertional angina I get walking Mom into Grand River for dialysis – that same 6/10 pressure that takes 10–15 minutes to settle. I didn’t want another scene like Zehrs on Ottawa St last May when the paramedics came.

Laura: We need to mention this to Dr. Nguyen and Dr. Patel. This plus you waking up 2–3 times a night with that warehouse nightmare and 5/10 chest heaviness isn’t nothing.

Michael: Yeah. I’ll log it in my symptom notebook. For now I’m home, feet up on the coffee table, CPAP ready for tonight, and I promise I won’t be the beer mule again.

Laura: Good. I’m bringing home low‑sodium soup. Stay put.

Sleep + CPAP + stress log – mid‑Jan 2026

Pattern since mid‑December 2025:

– Most nights I wake 2–3 times. Usual pattern: fall asleep with CPAP on around 11:00 p.m., wake up ~1:30 a.m. from the same nightmare – I’m on the TriCity warehouse floor arguing with Mark Jensen about missing the November 2025 overtime shifts because of chest pain, then collapsing. Wake with heart pounding, chest heaviness ~5/10, sweaty. Often rip the CPAP mask off.

– Takes 15–20 minutes sitting on the edge of the bed or in the hallway to calm down. Sometimes I walk to the kitchen, check my blood pressure and blood sugar, and double‑check the pillbox (beta‑blocker, ACE inhibitor, statin, aspirin, diabetes meds).

– Second or third wake‑up is usually milder palpitations, more like the 30–60 second “fluttering” I’ve been getting after weekly video sessions with Dr. Karen Li at Grand River Community Mental Health when we talk about money and Emily’s missed rent in November.

– On rehab days (St. Mary’s General Hospital, started 2025‑12‑15), afternoon fatigue is 7/10. If I also have to take Ryan to his probation check‑in at the Ontario Court of Justice on Frederick St, I’m completely drained and need 45–60 minutes lying down as soon as we get home.

To ask Dr. Nguyen next visit:

1. Is it okay that I’m still pulling the CPAP off halfway through the night?
2. Do we adjust sertraline 50 mg or add anything for the nightmares?
3. Any way these palpitations are more than just PVCs?

Short‑term goal: one night this week with only one wake‑up and keeping the CPAP on until 5 a.m.

Notes to self:

- Policy No: ELC-947521-39
- Phone Number Include Area Code: (519) 742-3894
- Patients Signature: Michael A. Thompson
- Date: 2026-01-15
- Date Of First Visit: 2025-11-28
- Date Of Most Recent Visit: 2026-01-10
- Frequency Of Visits Weekly: false
- Frequency Of Visits Monthly: true
- Frequency Of Visits Other Specify: false
- Date Of Discharge: 2025-12-02
